meaning: now I see, now I understand
lesson:
This is the Japanese "aha." Someone explains something and it clicks — なるほど. It literally breaks down as なる (to become) + ほど (to the extent that) — something like "it has become to that extent" — but nobody parses it that way anymore. It's a fixed expression that means "I see, that makes sense, I understand now."

Be careful with frequency. なるほど is a genuine response — you're acknowledging that what the other person said has landed. Using it as filler (nodding and saying なるほど every few seconds without actually processing anything) is a well-known conversational sin in Japanese. Some people find it slightly condescending when overused — as if you're evaluating what they said and granting approval. Use it when you genuinely mean "oh, I get it now."

In professional settings, なるほど can sound too casual when speaking to superiors. Safer alternatives: そうですか or おっしゃる通とおりですね.

つまり comes from the verb 詰つまる (つまる, to be packed, to be clogged, to reach a conclusion). When something 詰つまる, it gets compressed — squeezed down to its essence. That's what つまり does in a sentence: it compresses everything before it into a summary. "All that stuff I just said? Here's the point."

彼は毎日遅刻して、仕事もしない。つまり、クビだ。 (He's late every day and doesn't do his work. In short, he's fired.)

It's different from 要ようするに (ようするに), which also means "in short" but carries a slight impatience — "let me get to the point already." つまり is more neutral, more explanatory. You're clarifying, not rushing.

meaning: as it is, just like that
lesson:
その (that) + まま (as-is, unchanged state). Don't touch it. Don't change it. Leave it exactly how it is. そのままにしておいて (leave it as it is). そのまま食たべられる (you can eat it as-is — no cooking needed).

The power of そのまま is in what it prevents: action, change, interference. It's a freeze command. そのまま動うごかないで (don't move — stay exactly like that). In cooking, it means you don't need to prepare it further. In descriptions, it means a state was preserved: 昔のままだ uses まま alone, but そのまま points at a specific current state and says "keep it."

You already know まま from the vocabulary list. そのまま is its most common deployment in the wild.

Sharp edges. No ambiguity. That's はっきり. The doubled consonant っき gives it a crisp, definitive sound — appropriate for a word that means clear, distinct, definite.

It pairs naturally with する: はっきりする (to become clear). はっきり言いう (to say clearly, to speak bluntly). はっきり覚おぼえていない (I don't clearly remember). In the negative, it expresses vagueness — things you can't quite make out, memories you can't quite pin down.

はっきり言って (frankly speaking) is a common lead-in when someone is about to be direct — possibly uncomfortably so. Japanese communication often avoids bluntness, so announcing はっきり言って signals a deliberate break from that norm. What follows will be straight talk.

大たい (big) + 変へん (change, strange) = a big change, a serious situation. But 大変たいへん leads two different lives depending on how it's used, and mixing them up is a common mistake.

Life 1 — Adverb: "very." 大変嬉しいです (I'm very happy). Formal, slightly stiff — you'd use this in a speech or email, not chatting with friends. Think of it as the polite cousin of とても.

Life 2 — な-adjective/noun: "terrible, difficult, a big deal." 大変だ！ (this is terrible! / this is a lot!). 子育ては大変だ (raising kids is tough). 大変なことになった (things have gotten serious). This is the version you'll hear in daily life far more often.

The two meanings feel contradictory — "very" (just an intensifier) vs "terrible" (a judgment) — but the kanji holds them together: something big is happening. Whether that's big in degree (very) or big in consequence (terrible) depends on context.

Dictionaryたいへんだ
Negativeたいへんじゃない
Pastたいへんだった
Past negたいへんじゃなかった
Adverbたいへんに
て-formたいへんで

簡かん (simple, brief) + 単たん (single, simple). Double simplicity. The kanji 簡かん has the bamboo radical on top — bamboo strips were used as writing tablets in ancient China, and 簡かん originally referred to a single bamboo strip: brief, not complex. 単たん means "single, alone" — one thing, uncomplicated.

簡単かんたん is the everyday "easy" for tasks and concepts: この問題は簡単だ (this problem is easy). 簡単な料理 (a simple dish). It contrasts with 易しい (やさしい), which also means easy but emphasizes gentleness — easy in the sense of not being harsh or demanding. 簡単かんたん is about low complexity; 易しい is about low difficulty for the person doing it.

簡単かんたんに言いうと (to put it simply) is a useful phrase for introducing a simplified explanation.

Dictionaryかんたんだ
Negativeかんたんじゃない
Pastかんたんだった
Past negかんたんじゃなかった
Adverbかんたんに
て-formかんたんで

The dictionary form is 似にる (にる), but you'll almost never hear it that way. 似にている — the ongoing-state form — is the default because resemblance is a persistent condition, not a one-time action. Something doesn't "resemble" and then stop; it is resembling.

The kanji 似じ has 亻にんべん (person) and a right side that suggests following or imitating. A person who follows the pattern of another — a look-alike.

お母かあさんに似にている (looks like your/her mother). The particle is に — the person you resemble is marked with に, not を. You resemble toward someone, as if aiming at their appearance. この二つは似ている (these two are similar) — when comparing two things, no に is needed.

Particles: 似にる takes に to mark who/what is resembled. 父に似ている (I look like my father).

Causative に似させる: making something resemble something. Rare but valid: 本物に似させる (make it resemble the real thing).

一段 Ichidan自動詞
Dictionaryにるに
Negativeにないに
Politeにますに
Polite negにませんに
Pastにたに
Polite pastにましたに
て-formにてに

Polite volにましょうに

The kanji 驚きょう is visually loud — 23 strokes, with 馬うま (horse) at the bottom and 敬けい (respect, awe — used for sound) at the top. A startled horse. If you've ever seen a horse spook, the kanji's complexity starts to feel justified — surprise is sudden, chaotic, overwhelming.

おどろく covers the full range from mild surprise to genuine shock. それを聞いて驚いた (I was surprised to hear that). 驚くほど安い (surprisingly cheap — cheap to a startling degree). The 〜ほど pattern is common with this verb — using the surprise as a measure of intensity.

Notice: you don't surprise yourself. 驚おどろく is intransitive — you are surprised. To surprise someone else, you'd use 驚おどろかす (おどろかす) or びっくりさせる.

Particles: 驚おどろく takes に to mark what surprised you. 結果に驚いた (I was surprised at the result).

Causative を／に驚かせる: startling someone. みんなを驚かせた (I surprised everyone).

The sound says it all. I-ya — say it with a grimace and you've got the meaning. It's the face a child makes when they don't want to eat something. 嫌いや is blunt, visceral dislike — not intellectual disagreement, but a gut-level "no thank you."

嫌いやだ is how you flatly refuse or reject something in casual speech. 嫌なことがあった (something unpleasant happened). 嫌な予感がする (I have a bad feeling).

嫌 vs 嫌い: Both involve dislike, but they work differently. 嫌きらい (きらい) is "I dislike X" — a stated preference about a thing. 嫌いや (いや) is more immediate and emotional — "this is unpleasant, I don't want this, no." 嫌きらい categorizes. 嫌いや recoils.

Dictionaryいやだ
Negativeいやじゃない
Pastいやだった
Past negいやじゃなかった
Adverbいやに
て-formいやで

Two of the most aggressive-looking kanji in daily use. 喧けん is 口くち (mouth) plus 宣 (declare, proclaim) — a mouth making proclamations, being loud. 嘩か is 口くち plus 華 (showy, flashy) — a mouth making a spectacle of itself. Two kanji, two mouths, both screaming. The characters practically shout at you from the page.

喧嘩けんか covers everything from a shouting match between friends to a physical fight in the street. 喧嘩けんかする (to fight, to argue). 兄弟喧嘩 (sibling fight). 夫婦喧嘩ふうふげんか (marital argument). The word doesn't specify violence — it could be verbal or physical. Context determines severity.

喧嘩けんかを売うる (to pick a fight — literally "sell a fight") and 喧嘩を買う (to accept a challenge — literally "buy a fight"). The fight is treated as a commodity being offered and accepted. Vivid.

遅おくれる means to fall behind schedule — to be late, to lag, to arrive after the expected time. 電車でんしゃが遅おくれている (the train is running late). 授業に遅れた (I was late to class). The に marks what you're late for.

The related adjective 遅おそい (おそい) means "slow" or "late" as a static quality. 遅おくれる is the process of becoming late — the moment you fall behind. A clock that's 遅れている is running slow (it has fallen behind real time). A person who is 遅おそい is generally slow-moving.

The transitive counterpart is 遅らせる (おくらせる, to delay something): 出発を遅らせる (to delay the departure).

Particles: 遅おくれる takes に to mark what you're late for. 会議に遅れた (I was late to the meeting).

Causative を／に遅れさせる: delaying someone or making them late. 渋滞が彼を遅れさせた (the traffic jam made him late).

Written in hiragana or katakana, though the kanji 人参にんじん exists — literally "person" + "ginseng." The name reflects the carrot's arrival in Japan through Chinese medicine, where both carrots and ginseng were valued root vegetables. The "person" kanji comes from ginseng roots resembling a human figure — and the name stuck to the orange vegetable that arrived later.

にんじん is one of the first vegetables Japanese children learn to either love or hate. The expression にんじんが嫌きらい is almost a cliche of childhood pickiness. It's a staple of カレー (curry), 肉じゃが (meat and potato stew), and most home cooking.

The name comes from Jakarta — specifically ジャガタラいも, "Jakarta potato," because Dutch traders brought potatoes to Japan via Jakarta (then called Jacarta) in the 17th century. Over time, ジャガタラ shortened to ジャガ, and いも (potato/tuber) stayed. The word is a compressed history of colonial trade routes.

Usually written in katakana or hiragana. The kanji 馬鈴薯ばれいしょ (ばれいしょ) exists but is mainly used in agriculture and formal writing. In a grocery store or kitchen, it's always ジャガイモ.

ジャガイモ vs さつまいも (sweet potato): both are いも, but the regular potato is the one that came via Jakarta. The sweet potato came through Satsuma (now Kagoshima). Japanese named them by origin.

Written in katakana, hiragana, or with the kanji 茄子なす. The word is ancient Japanese — not a Chinese import — and the name might come from the idea of summer (夏なつ, なつ) vegetables, since eggplant is a quintessential summer crop.

Eggplant in Japan tends to be slimmer and more tender than the large globe eggplants common in Western countries. Japanese preparations — 焼きなす (grilled eggplant), なすの味噌炒め (miso-stir-fried eggplant), 漬物 (pickled) — depend on this variety.

There's a famous New Year's saying: 一富士、二鷹、三なすび (first Fuji, second hawk, third eggplant) — the three luckiest things to dream about on January 1st. Why eggplant? One theory: なす sounds like 成す (to accomplish). A pun woven into tradition.

The kanji 薬缶やかん tells an unexpected story: 薬やく (medicine) + 缶かん (can, container). A medicine container? The word traces back to a vessel used for boiling medicinal herbs — a specialized pot for decoctions. Over centuries, as the pot became general-purpose for boiling water, the name stuck even though nobody was making medicine in it anymore.

A やかん is specifically the kind that goes on a stove — a traditional kettle with a spout, usually metal. It's distinct from an electric kettle, which is 電気ポット or 電気ケトル. When Japanese people say やかんでお湯を沸かす (boil water in the kettle), they mean the stovetop kind — the one that whistles.

話はなす (to speak) + 合あう (to match, to do mutually). The 〜合う suffix is one of the most useful verb-building tools in Japanese: it turns a one-directional action into a reciprocal one. 話はなす = one person talks. 話はなし合あう = people talk to each other, back and forth — a discussion. Other examples: 助け合う (help each other), 見つめ合う (gaze at each other).

話はなし合あう implies working something out together — it's not just chatting. 二人でよく話し合った方がいい (you two should discuss it thoroughly). この問題について話し合おう (let's discuss this problem). It's constructive and mutual. The noun form 話し合い (はなしあい) means a discussion or negotiation.

Particles: 話はなし合あう takes について or を for the topic. この件について話し合う (discuss this matter). と marks who you discuss with: 彼と話し合う.

Passive 話し合われる: the topic is being discussed. この問題は会議で話し合われた (this issue was discussed at the meeting).

Causative に話し合わせる: making people discuss. 二人に話し合わせた (I had the two of them discuss it).

meaning: to leave behind, save, not finish
lesson:
You know 残のこる (のこる, to remain — intransitive). 残のこす is its transitive partner: you leave something behind, deliberately or not. ご飯を残す (to leave food uneaten). 記録きろくを残のこす (to leave behind a record). 名を残す (to leave one's name — to be remembered).

Leaving food on your plate (ご飯を残す) is culturally loaded in Japan. The expectation is that you finish what you're served. Children hear 残さないで食べなさい (eat it all, don't leave any) constantly. Leaving food implies waste or ingratitude. The verb carries that weight even when the context is neutral.

Particles: 残のこす takes を — the thing left behind is your direct object. 子供たちに財産を残す (leave the children an inheritance).

Potential が残せる: が — いい結果が残せた (I was able to leave behind good results).

Passive に残される: に marks who left you behind. みんなに残された (I was left behind by everyone).

Causative に…を残させる: に marks the person, を marks the thing. 生徒に宿題を残させた (had the students leave homework behind — incomplete).

meaning: to treat, host (someone to a meal)
lesson:
You already know ごちそう — a feast, a treat. Add する and it becomes the verb: to treat someone to food or drink. 今日きょうは私わたしがごちそうする (today I'll treat / it's my treat). 先輩せんぱいにごちそうしてもらった (my senior treated me).

The kanji behind ごちそう are 御馳走ごちそう — 馳ち (to gallop) + 走そう (to run), with the honorific 御. The original image: running around on horseback to gather ingredients for a guest. The effort of preparation, not just the food itself, is what the word honors. That's why ごちそうさまでした (thank you for the meal) isn't just thanking someone for food — it's acknowledging all the work that went into it.

Particles: に marks the person treated. 友達にごちそうする (treat a friend). を marks what you treat them to: 友達に昼ご飯をごちそうする.

Irregular (する)他動詞

Same pronunciation as 会あう (to meet), different kanji, different meaning. 合あう is about things coming together correctly — fitting, matching, being in agreement. サイズが合あう (the size fits). 答えが合っている (the answer is correct — it matches). 話が合う (we get along — our conversations click).

The kanji 合ごう shows a lid (亼) coming down onto a container (口) — two things meeting and fitting together. That image extends everywhere: 合計 (total — things combined), 合格ごうかく (passing — meeting the standard), 合う itself.

As a suffix (〜合う), it makes verbs reciprocal — we covered this in 話し合う. Here as a standalone verb, it's simpler: do things match?

Particles: 合あう takes が — the thing that fits is the subject. この靴が足に合わない (these shoes don't fit my feet). に marks what it fits with/to. と marks what it matches: この色はシャツと合う.

Causative を合わせる: this is actually a separate common word — 合わせる (あわせる, to match, to combine, to adjust). 時計を合わせる (set a clock — make it match the correct time).

meaning: to hit, strike, win, be correct, succeed
lesson:
当あたる has a deceptively wide meaning range, but every use traces back to one idea: something meets its target. A ball 当あたる when it hits. A lottery 当あたる when you win. A prediction 当あたる when it comes true. Food 当あたる when it hits your stomach wrong (food poisoning). Sunlight 当あたる when it strikes your skin. Target met, every time.

The kanji 当とう originally depicted an equal, corresponding match — something fitting precisely where it should. That's why 当然とうぜん (とうぜん, naturally) uses it: "hitting the mark of what should be."

宝くじに当たった (I won the lottery). 日が当たる (sunlight hits — a sunny spot). 彼に当たるな (don't take it out on him — don't let your frustration "hit" him). Learn the core image — contact with a target — and the meanings unfold naturally.

Particles: 当あたる takes に to mark the target or what you hit/won. ボールが頭に当たった (the ball hit my head). くじに当たる (win a lottery).

Causative を／に当たらせる: making something hit or assigning someone to a task. 新人に担当を当たらせる is unusual — you'd more likely use 当てる (あてる, transitive: to aim, to assign).

集あつまる is the intransitive partner of 集あつめる (あつめる, to gather/collect — transitive). Things 集あつまる on their own. People 集あつめる them. 人が集まった (people gathered) vs 人を集めた (I gathered people).

The kanji 集しゅう is a bird (隹, an old radical meaning "short-tailed bird") landing on a tree (木). Birds flocking to a tree — gathering. It's one of the more pictorially satisfying kanji: you can almost see the flock settling onto branches.

駅前に人が集まっている (people are gathered in front of the station). 注目が集まる (attention gathers — something draws attention). お金が集まった (money was collected/raised). The verb works for physical gatherings and abstract concentrations alike.

Particles: 集あつまる takes が for what gathers, and に for where. 公園に人が集まった (people gathered at the park).

Causative を／に集まらせる: making people gather. を for coercive — 生徒を集まらせた (made the students gather). In practice, 集あつめる is more natural for this meaning.

場ば is ground (土つち) plus a phonetic element that originally meant "a cleared area" — a patch of earth made ready for something. 所 you already know as ところ (place). Together: a specific, defined location. Not vague like ところ can be — 場所ばしょ is always a concrete, physical spot you could point to on a map.

場所 vs ところ: ところ drifts into abstraction ("the point where I was about to leave," "my weak spot"). 場所ばしょ stays grounded. 場所を決める (decide on a location). 場所ばしょを取とる (take up space). 場所がない (there's no room). When you need to talk about physical space — where to meet, where to sit, where something is — 場所ばしょ is the word.

The 場ば kanji does heavy lifting in compounds: 工場 (こうじょう, factory — a work-ground), 駐車場 (ちゅうしゃじょう, parking lot), 遊び場 (あそびば, playground). Every one is "the ground designated for [X]." Once you see 場ば as "prepared ground," these compounds assemble themselves.

The kanji 海うみ is water (氵さんずい) plus 毎まい, which originally depicted a woman with elaborate hair — meaning "every, each." Water that is everywhere, that covers everything: the sea. It's a satisfying etymology because the ocean really is the body of water that seems to go on without end in every direction.

Sound anchor: u-mi. The う rolls up like a wave, and み falls back down. Say it slowly and feel the rise and fall. Now compare with 湖 (みずうみ, lake) — same うみ at the end, because a lake is also a body of water, but with みず (water) in front, specifying what kind.

海うみ generates a whole family of compounds you'll encounter constantly. 海外かいがい (かいがい, overseas) is literally "sea-outside" — beyond the water. 海岸 (かいがん, coast) is "sea-shore." Japan is an island nation, so the sea is never far from the language. 海の日 (Marine Day) is a national holiday. The sea isn't just geography in Japanese — it's identity.

少しょう (few, little) plus 年ねん (year): a person of few years. The word has a specific emotional register — it's the word newspapers use for minors in crime reports, the word manga magazines put in their titles (少年ジャンプ), and the word adults use when reminiscing about youth. You wouldn't call a kid playing in a park a 少年しょうねん to their face. It's a label applied from outside, often with a tinge of nostalgia or formality.

Its counterpart is 少女しょうじょ (しょうじょ, girl) — same construction, same register. For everyday "boy" and "girl," Japanese uses 男の子 and 女の子. 少年しょうねん and 少女しょうじょ are the literary versions, the ones that show up in titles and formal contexts.

The age range matters: roughly 7 to 18. Below that, it's 子供. Above that, you're a 若者 (わかもの, youth) or just an adult. 少年しょうねん occupies that specific window where you're no longer a small child but not yet independent.

The kanji 孫まご is 子こ (child) plus 系 (thread, lineage) compressed. A child connected by a thread of descent — not your child, but your child's child. The thread skips a generation.

まご is one of those warm family words that Japanese culture wraps in affection. The polite form お孫さん (おまごさん) is what you use when asking about someone else's grandchild — because in Japanese, you always honor other people's family members with polite prefixes while keeping your own humble. Your grandchild is 孫まご. Their grandchild is お孫さん.

The compound 孫まごの手て (a backscratcher — literally "grandchild's hand") tells you everything about the cultural expectation: grandchildren scratch your back for you. It's the most useful hand in the house.

Japanese has three words for "student" and they aren't interchangeable. 生徒せいと is K-12 — elementary through high school. 学生がくせい (がくせい) is university. 児童じどう (じどう) is the legal/formal term for children. Use the wrong one and you'll tag someone with the wrong age instantly.

生せい (life, birth, raw) plus 徒と (follower, disciple). A 徒と is someone who follows a master — it's the same character in 徒歩 (とほ, on foot) and 徒弟 (とてい, apprentice). A 生徒せいと is literally a "living follower" — someone actively under a teacher's guidance.

You'll hear 生徒せいと constantly in school contexts: 生徒会 (student council), 生徒指導 (student guidance). If a Japanese person says they're a 生徒せいと, they're in high school or below. If they say 学生がくせい, they're in college. The distinction is sharp.

This is a clipped form of 高等学校こうとうがっこう (こうとうがっこう, "high-level school"). Japanese loves these abbreviations: take the first kanji of each two-character block and smash them together. 高こう (high) + 校 (school) = 高校こうこう. Same pattern: 中学 from 中学校ちゅうがっこう (middle school), 小学 from 小学校 (elementary school), 大学 from 大学校 (university).

In Japan, high school isn't compulsory — middle school is the last required stage. But over 98% of students go anyway, making 高校こうこう essentially universal. The entrance exam (高校入試) is the first major academic pressure point in a Japanese student's life, often more stressful than college admissions. When people say 高校時代 (high school days), there's usually a specific emotional weight behind it.

年とし (year/age) + 上うえ (above) = above in age. Its opposite is 年下としした (としした, younger). These aren't just descriptors — they're social sorting mechanisms. Knowing whether someone is 年上としうえ or 年下としした determines which speech register you use, who pours the drinks, who enters the room first.

Japanese culture has 先輩せんぱい (せんぱい, senior) and 後輩こうはい (こうはい, junior) for formal hierarchies at school and work. 年上としうえ is the more personal, everyday version — it's about actual age, not institutional rank. 彼女は三つ年上だ (she's three years older). In dating contexts, 年上としうえ comes up constantly — age gaps matter more conversationally in Japan than in many Western cultures.

卒そつ originally meant a foot soldier — someone who has completed their service. 業ぎょう means work, achievement, or studies. Together: finishing your studies, your service complete. The military metaphor isn't accidental. Traditional East Asian education was conceived as a form of disciplined service.

卒業そつぎょう has expanded beyond schools. You'll hear AKB48を卒業する (graduate from AKB48 — leave the idol group), or casually, タバコを卒業した (I "graduated" from smoking — I quit). The word carries a nuance of completion and moving on that "quit" doesn't. You didn't just stop. You finished that chapter.

卒業式 (そつぎょうしき, graduation ceremony) is a major cultural event in Japan — cherry blossoms, tears, second buttons given to crushes. The ceremony is the emotional capstone, not an afterthought.

運うん (carry, transport, luck) + 動どう (move). Carrying yourself into motion. The word covers two distinct domains: physical exercise (運動うんどうする = to exercise, 運動不足 = lack of exercise) and organized movements (市民運動 = citizens' movement, 選挙運動 = election campaign). The connecting thread: bodies in coordinated motion, whether at a gym or in the streets.

For most learners, the daily-life meaning dominates. 運動しないと (if you don't exercise...) is a sentence you'll hear from doctors, parents, and health-conscious friends. 運動会うんどうかい (うんどうかい, sports day) is an annual school event where the entire student body competes in relay races, tug-of-war, and choreographed group dances. It's a bigger deal than you'd expect.

選せん (choose, select) + 手しゅ (hand, person who does something). A chosen hand — someone selected to represent. The word implies competition and selection: you don't just play a sport, you were picked. That's why 選手せんしゅ sounds more formal than casual. A kid kicking a ball in the park isn't a 選手せんしゅ. A kid on the school team is.

In sports broadcasting, 選手せんしゅ is appended to names the way English uses "player": 大谷選手 (Player Ohtani). It's the standard respectful way to refer to athletes in news and commentary.

The 手しゅ suffix appears in many role words: 運転手 (driver), 歌手 (singer), 相手 (partner/opponent). The hand that drives, the hand that sings, the hand that faces you. 手しゅ here doesn't mean "hand" literally — it means "the person who does the thing."

映えい means to project, reflect, or shine — it's the character in 反映はんえい (はんえい, reflection). 画が means picture or painting. A projected picture: cinema. The etymology captures what movies literally were when the word was coined — pictures thrown onto a screen by light.

Japanese cinema vocabulary is its own little world. 映画館えいがかん (えいがかん, movie theater). 映画監督 (えいがかんとく, film director). But in conversation, people often just say 映画を見る or 映画を観る (watch a movie) — note that 観る (みる) is the "watch for enjoyment" kanji, distinct from the everyday 見る.

Sound anchor: ei-ga — think of it as "A-ga" where the "A" is the grade and が is the subject marker. "A is the picture." 映画えいが, A-picture, the A-grade projected image. Not etymologically true, but once the sound clicks, the word stays.

英えい means brilliant, outstanding, hero. 語ご means language. "The brilliant language"? Not quite — 英えい is the character Japan chose to represent England (英国 = England/UK), likely a phonetic borrowing for the "ei" sound in "England." So 英語えいご is simply "English-country language."

This pattern applies to every language: 日本語 (Japanese), 中国語 (Chinese), フランス語 (French). Country name + 語ご = that country's language. Simple, productive, no exceptions.

In Japan, 英語えいご carries cultural weight beyond just "the English language." It's a school subject that students study for years, a source of anxiety for many adults, and a marker of cosmopolitan sophistication. 英語ができる (can do English) is something people say with either pride or apology. The relationship between Japan and English is complicated, and the word 英語えいご often summons that whole complicated history.

Here's a false friend that trips up Chinese speakers: in Chinese, 手紙てがみ means "toilet paper." In Japanese, it means a letter you mail. Same characters, wildly different meanings. If a Chinese-speaking friend asks for 手紙てがみ, they might not want stationery.

The Japanese meaning makes sense if you think about it historically: 手て (hand) + 紙かみ (paper) = paper written by hand. Before typing existed, every letter was handwritten, so "hand-paper" was simply what you called written correspondence.

Even in the digital age, 手紙てがみ specifically means a physical letter — paper, envelope, stamp. Email is メール. A text message is メッセージ. The word hasn't expanded to cover electronic communication the way English "letter" sometimes does. In Japan, sending someone a handwritten 手紙てがみ is still a meaningful gesture — it signals care, formality, and the investment of time.

動どう (move) + 物ぶつ (thing). A moving thing. Compare with 植物 (しょくぶつ, plant — "planted thing"). Japanese divides the living world by the simplest possible criterion: does it move on its own, or does it stay put? Animals move. Plants are planted. The logic is ancient — the same division exists in many languages — but it's unusually transparent in the kanji.

動物園どうぶつえん (どうぶつえん, zoo) is literally "moving-thing garden." 植物園 (しょくぶつえん, botanical garden) is "planted-thing garden." The parallel is clean.

The 物ぶつ suffix is one of the most productive in Japanese. It turns concepts into concrete things: 食べ物 (food), 飲み物 (drink), 買い物 (shopping), 建物 (building). Once you recognize 物ぶつ as "the thing version of [X]," compound words start unpacking themselves.

音おと is 立りつ (stand) over 日にち (sun/day), but the bottom component was originally a mouth with something inside it — a sound trying to get out. The modern form disguises this, but the meaning is clear: something vibrating, something audible.

音 vs 声: Both relate to what you hear, but they split the world cleanly. 音おと (おと) is any sound — rain on a roof, a car horn, a piano note. 声 (こえ) is specifically a voice, a sound made by a living throat. 足音あしおと (footsteps) uses 音おと. 歌声 (singing voice) uses 声. If it comes from vocal cords, it's 声. Everything else is 音おと.

The on'yomi reading おん shows up in music-related compounds: 音楽おんがく (おんがく, music), 発音はつおん (はつおん, pronunciation), 騒音 (そうおん, noise pollution). The kun'yomi おと is for standalone use: 大きな音 (a loud sound).

Sea-outside. 海かい (sea) + 外がい (outside) = beyond the ocean. For an island nation, "abroad" literally means "past the water." There's no way to leave Japan without crossing the sea (or flying over it), and the language encodes that geography directly.

海外かいがい vs 外国 (がいこく): both mean "foreign" but with different emphasis. 海外かいがい emphasizes the act of going or being away — 海外旅行かいがいりょこう (overseas travel), 海外生活 (life abroad). 外国 emphasizes the otherness of the place — 外国語 (foreign language), 外国人 (foreigner). 海外かいがい looks outward from Japan. 外国 looks at the foreign place itself.

外国がいこく (foreign country) + 人じん (person). Structurally transparent, culturally loaded. This is the neutral, polite term for a non-Japanese person. In everyday casual speech, you'll hear 外人 (がいじん) — same meaning, shorter — but some people find it abrupt or mildly rude, so 外国人がいこくじん is the safer choice.

The more recent alternative is 外国の方 (がいこくのかた), which is softer and more respectful — 方 is the polite word for "person." You'll see this in official contexts like government forms and public announcements.

Japan's relationship with the concept of "foreigner" is distinctive. The word 外国人がいこくじん can be applied even to long-term residents, even to people born in Japan who aren't ethnically Japanese. It marks an outside/inside boundary that doesn't always correlate with actual foreignness. Understanding the word means understanding that 内 and 外 (inside and outside) run deep in the culture.

帰き (return) + 国こく (country). Going back to your country. Simple enough, but notice the assumption baked into the word: you have one country, and returning to it is a single defined action. 帰国する means you're heading home for good or for a significant stay — not a weekend trip.

帰国子女 (きこくしじょ, returnee children) is a specific cultural term for Japanese children who lived abroad during formative years and then returned. These kids often struggle to fit back in, having absorbed foreign habits and sometimes better English than Japanese. The word 帰国きこく echoes through their entire identity — they're defined by the act of coming back.

Compare: 帰る (go home — daily) vs 帰国する (return to one's country — international). Same 帰き kanji, vastly different scale.

meaning: they (group of males or mixed gender)
lesson:
彼かれ (he) + ら (pluralizing suffix) = they. The ら suffix works like a pluralizer for pronouns: 僕ぼくら (we, male casual), 君ら (you guys). But unlike English "they," 彼かれら defaults to male or mixed groups. An all-female group would more naturally be 彼女ら or 彼女たち, though in practice, 彼かれら often covers everyone regardless.

Like most Japanese pronouns, 彼かれら is used less than you'd expect. Japanese sentences drop the subject whenever context makes it clear, and third-person plural references often use the group's name instead: あの人たち (those people) or 学生たち (the students). 彼かれら is slightly bookish — more common in writing than in everyday speech.

Both kanji share the 木き (tree/wood) radical on the left, because the earliest machines were wooden: looms, pulleys, watermill wheels. 機き originally meant a loom's mechanism — the intricate wooden device that turns thread into fabric. 械かい meant a tool or implement. Together, they point at anything with moving parts designed to do work.

Watch out for the homophone: 機会 (きかい, opportunity/chance) sounds identical. Context always disambiguates, but in conversation you might do a double-take. 機械が壊れた (the machine broke) vs 機会があれば (if there's a chance) — very different situations.

Modern Japanese uses 機械きかい broadly: 機械的 (きかいてき, mechanical) can describe both a literal machine and a person going through the motions without feeling. "He's being mechanical about it" works the same way in both languages.

基き is a foundation stone — the radical 土つち (earth) is at the bottom, supporting everything above. 本ほん is root, origin, base. A foundation's foundation. The word doubles down on "ground level" to emphasize: this is where everything starts.

基本的に (きほんてきに, basically) is a word you'll use and hear daily. It softens statements the way English "basically" does: 基本的に賃成です (basically, I agree). The 的 suffix turns nouns into な-adjectives, and に makes it an adverb. This 〜的に pattern is extremely productive.

基本きほん also shows up in practical contexts: 基本給 (base salary), 基本情報 (basic information), 基本料金 (base rate/fee). Wherever English uses "basic" or "base," Japanese likely uses 基本きほん.

今こん (now) + 度ど (time, occasion). The "now" occasion. But here's the thing that confuses learners: 今度こんど can mean either "this time" (the current occasion) or "next time" (the upcoming occasion), depending on context. 今度は大丈夫 (this time it's fine). 今度飲みに行こう (let's go drinking next time).

The ambiguity isn't a flaw — it reflects how Japanese thinks about time. "This time" and "next time" are both "the now-occasion" as opposed to past occasions. Whether that occasion is happening right now or is yet to come, it's the current one on the timeline.

Warning: 今度飲みに行こう is one of those vague social promises that may or may not lead to actual plans. It's the Japanese equivalent of "we should hang out sometime" — sincere in feeling, noncommittal in practice. If someone says 今度こんど without pinning down a date, don't hold your breath.

最さい (most, utmost) + 後ご (after, behind). The utmost after — the very last. Nothing comes behind it. 最後のチャンス (last chance). 最後さいごまで (until the end). 最後に (lastly, finally).

The 最さい prefix is a superlative marker — "the most." It builds a whole family: 最初 (さいしょ, first), 最近 (さいきん, recently — "most recent"), 最高 (さいこう, best/highest), 最低 (さいてい, worst/lowest). Recognizing 最さい = "most" unlocks all of these at once.

最後さいご and 最初さいしょ are a natural pair — bookends. If you learn one, learn the other. They're the first and last words you need for talking about beginnings and endings.

最初さいしょは嫌きらいだった (at first, I hated it). That sentence pattern — 最初さいしょは followed by a past feeling, then a shift — is everywhere in Japanese storytelling. "At first I thought X, but then..." The word practically demands a plot twist after it.

The kanji 初しょ shows a knife (刀) cutting into cloth (衣 compressed) — the first incision. The moment the blade touches fabric, something irreversibly new begins. That visceral image is why 初しょ feels different from merely "first" — it carries the sense of a fresh start, a threshold crossed. 初心 (しょしん, beginner's mind) in Zen is literally "first-cut mind" — the state before habits and assumptions calcify.

Don't confuse 最初さいしょ with 初め (はじめ). 最初さいしょ pinpoints the first moment: 最初の一歩 (the very first step). 初め stretches across the opening phase: 初めから終わりまで (from beginning to end). One is a point. The other is a period.

準じゅん means level, standard, quasi-. 備び means equip, furnish, prepare. Getting equipped to the required level: preparation. The word suggests thoroughness — not just getting ready, but getting ready properly.

準備じゅんびする is the verb form, and you'll use it constantly. 準備じゅんびができた (preparations are done / I'm ready) is one of the most satisfying sentences to say. 準備中 (じゅんびちゅう, in preparation) is a sign you'll see on stores before opening time.

準備じゅんび vs 用意 (ようい): both translate to "preparation," but 準備じゅんび is broader — getting ready mentally, physically, logistically. 用意 is specifically about having things arranged and ready to use: 用意してください (please have it ready / please prepare it). 準備じゅんび is the process. 用意 is the result.

The kanji 進しん has the 辻つじ radical (road/movement) with 隹ふるとり (a bird) inside. A bird on the road, moving forward. There's an older interpretation: the bird is a dove or sparrow, creatures that only walk in one direction — they hop forward, never backward. That's 進すすむ: forward movement with no retreat.

進すすむ is intransitive — things advance on their own. Work progresses: 仕事が進む. A clock runs fast: 時計とけいが進すすんでいる. A conversation moves forward: 話が進む. Its transitive partner is 進すすめる (すすめる) — you push something forward, you advance a plan.

Particles: 進すすむ takes に or へ for direction — 前まえに進すすむ (move forward). It can also take が when the subject is what's advancing: 計画が進む (the plan advances).

Passive に進まれる: rare, since it's intransitive. If used, に marks the agent: 敵に進まれた (the enemy advanced on us).

Causative を／に進ませる: making someone advance. を for coercive, に for permissive. 軍を進ませる (advance the troops).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 進める (すすめる)
Dictionaryすすむに／へ
Negativeすすまないに／へ
Politeすすみますに／へ
Polite negすすみませんに／へ
Pastすすんだに／へ
Polite pastすすみましたに／へ
て-formすすんでに／へ
Volitionalすすもうに／へ
Polite volすすみましょうに／へ
Passiveすすまれるに／へ
Causativeすすませるを／に
Caus-passすすませられるに
Potentialすすめるに／へ
Imperativeすすめに／へ
Imp なさいすすみなさいに／へ
Cond ～ばすすめばに／へ
Cond ～たらすすんだらに／へ

直ちょく (straight, direct, fix) + 接せつ (touch, contact). Straight contact — no intermediary, no buffer. When you meet someone 直接ちょくせつ, you meet them face to face. When you say something 直接ちょくせつ, you say it to the person, not through someone else.

In a culture that values indirectness, 直接ちょくせつ carries weight. 直接言う (say it directly) implies courage — or bluntness, depending on tone. 直接的な表現 (direct expression) can be a compliment or a warning, because Japanese communication often prefers the indirect route. Choosing 直接ちょくせつ is choosing to skip the soft packaging.

The opposite is 間接かんせつ (かんせつ, indirect) — 間 (between) replacing 直ちょく (straight). Contact through a middle layer. 間接的に聞いた (I heard indirectly — through the grapevine).

特とく means special, unique — it's a bull (牛うし) with a mark on it, one that stands out from the herd. Add に to make it an adverb and you get "specially, particularly" — singling something out from the crowd.

特に is one of those adverbs that makes your Japanese sound immediately more natural. Instead of just listing things you like, you can highlight one: 日本料理が好きです。特に寿司が好きです (I like Japanese food. Especially sushi). The word creates hierarchy in a flat list.

Also common in negative form: 特にない (nothing in particular). Asked what you want for dinner? 特にない. It's the polite Japanese shrug.

meaning: to be received, be delivered, reach, arrive
lesson:
届とどく is an arrival verb — but from the perspective of the destination, not the traveler. A package arrives: 荷物にもつが届とどいた. A letter reaches you: 手紙が届いた. Your voice carries to someone: 声が届く. The thing being delivered is always the subject, not the person receiving.

Its transitive partner is 届とどける (とどける) — to deliver, to submit, to file a report. 届く = it arrives. 届ける = you send it. The pair is clean: something reaches on its own (届とどく) vs. you make it reach (届とどける).

Figuratively, 届とどく means to reach in the sense of being sufficient: 手が届かない (can't reach — literally, your hand doesn't arrive). 気持ちが届く (one's feelings reach someone) — a beautiful phrase for when your sincerity gets through.

Particles: 届とどく takes が for what arrives and に for where it arrives. 荷物が家に届いた (the package arrived at the house). The thing arriving is the subject, not the object.

Passive に届かれる: uncommon since 届とどく is already intransitive, but possible in indirect passive: 夜中に荷物に届かれて起こされた (a package arrived in the middle of the night and woke me up).

Causative を／に届かせる: making something reach. 声を届かせる (make your voice carry).

五段 Godan自動詞 — pair: 届ける (とどける)

Negativeとどかないが
Politeとどきますが
Polite negとどきませんが
Pastとどいたが
Polite pastとどきましたが
て-formとどいてが
Volitionalとどこうが
Polite volとどきましょうが
